title: London Exhibition Tells Stories of Southern Lebanon's People
summary: Have you ever felt like nobody is listening to your story? That's how many people in southern Lebanon feel. But a new exhibition in London called "Forget Me Not" is making sure their voices are heard. The show features videos, photos, and drawings that tell the history of this region. It shows how people have survived wars and difficult times. One video shows a person saying, "We are attached to this land just like a tree is rooted in its soil." That means they love their home and won't leave, no matter what. The exhibition was created by two people who have family roots in southern Lebanon: Rasha Kotaiche and Ali Abou Khalil. They wanted to share the real stories of their people—not just what you see on the news. There are drawings made by Lebanese schoolchildren celebrating their country's independence day. There are also photos of pomegranates and oranges from the region. The exhibition reminds us that behind every news story about war, there are real people with real lives, hopes, and dreams.
why_it_matters: This exhibition shows that even when big countries fight, regular people—just like you and your family—are the ones who feel it most, and their stories deserve to be told.

background_read: Southern Lebanon has experienced many wars and a long occupation.
Many people from that region feel that the news doesn't tell their true stories.
This exhibition uses art to help people around the world understand what life is like there.
Article_Structure: WHO: Rasha Kotaiche and Ali Abou Khalil, creators of the exhibition.
WHAT: An exhibition called 'Forget Me Not' that shares stories of southern Lebanon through videos, photos, and drawings.
WHY: To give a voice to people from southern Lebanon and show their deep connection to their land.

perspectives.description: The exhibition allows people from southern Lebanon to share their own stories and be heard.
Some may argue that a short exhibition in London cannot fully capture the reality of life in southern Lebanon.
Art exhibitions can help raise awareness about neglected regions and humanize complex conflicts.
